- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: It exercises the actual `monitorWebhook` request handler without spinning the full provider lifecycle.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): None.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Feishu webhook mode now returns `404 Not Found` for requests sent to paths other than the configured `webhookPath` (on the same host/port).

target.title: Adding OpenRouter support for allowedModels
target.body:
🤖 AI-assisted PR, validated by a human (me) — tested locally, all openrouter extension tests pass.

  ## Background

I'm the founder of [ClawOps](https://clawops.io), a managed AI assistant platform built on top of OpenClaw. While there are other hosting options for OpenClaw, we're not just a VPS provider: our goal is to give non-technical users immediate, zero-config access to personal AI agents. Let's face it, for technical users it's easy enough to set up and configure OpenClaw agents, but there is still loads of config tweaking behind the scenes to optimise memory, capability and cost. We want to take that out of the equation.

We believe personal (and business) agents will become as common as Netflix subscriptions.

One of our core differentiators is [Model Stacks](https://docs.clawops.io/features/model-stacks/). The idea is users operate on cost effective LLM stacks (e.g. Big Tech, Indie, Premium), letting users get intelligent routing without needing to understand the underlying model landscape.

In addition we have [Smart Switching](https://docs.clawops.io/features/smart-switching/), which automatically changes the stack based on the user's credits. This means that even when they run low on credits the agent stays on and responsive using free models.

OpenRouter already supports automatic model selection for cost optimisation, see [Using Auto Model for Cost Optimization](https://openrouter.ai/docs/guides/coding-agents/openclaw-integration#using-auto-model-for-cost-optimization). OpenRouter also supports further config to use `auto` with constraints to specific models via the [`plugins` API](https://openrouter.ai/docs/guides/routing/routers/auto-router). However, this config is not currently supported in OpenClaw, this PR aims to solve that.

  ---

  ## Summary

  Config example (exact model IDs):
 ```yaml
  model: openrouter/openrouter/auto
  params:
    autoRouter:
      allowedModels:
        - anthropic/claude-haiku-4-5
        - google/gemini-2.5-flash
        - openai/gpt-5-nano
```

  Config example (wildcard patterns):
 ```yaml
  model: openrouter/openrouter/auto
  params:
    autoRouter:
      allowedModels:
        - anthropic/*
        - google/*
```

  - Problem: openrouter/openrouter/auto routes across all OpenRouter models with no way to constrain the pool, making it unsuitable for cost-controlled or curated deployments.
  - Why it matters: Users running fixed model stacks (e.g. Big Tech / Indie / Premium tier pools) want intelligent per-prompt routing within their stack, not across the entire marketplace.
  - What changed: Added injectAutoRouterPlugin() in extensions/openrouter/index.ts that injects plugins: [{ id: "auto-router", allowed_models: [...] }] into the request payload when params.autoRouter.allowedModels is set. 3 new unit tests added. Docs updated.
  - What did NOT change: No core files touched. No type schema changes (params is already Record<string, unknown>). No behavior change when autoRouter is absent — existing configs unaffected.

 ###  Change Type

  - Feature 
  - Docs

  ###  Scope 

  - Integrations

  ### Linked Issue/PR

  None

 ### User-visible / Behavior Changes

 New optional config param params.autoRouter.allowedModels on the openrouter/openrouter/auto model. Accepts exact model IDs (anthropic/claude-haiku-4-5) or wildcard patterns (anthropic/*). When set, auto-routing is constrained to the provided model list. When absent, behavior is identical to current, no change to defaults.

  ### Security Impact 

  - New permissions/capabilities? No
  - Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
  - New/changed network calls? No — same OpenRouter endpoint, additional field in existing payload
  - Command/tool execution surface changed? No
  - Data access scope changed? No

 ### Repro + Verification

  Environment

  - OS: Windows 11 (WSL2)
  - Runtime/container: Node.js (pnpm)
  - Model/provider: openrouter/openrouter/auto via OpenRouter API
  - Integration/channel: Direct API / Telegram
  - Relevant config (redacted): see config examples above

 ### Steps

  1. Set model to openrouter/openrouter/auto with params.autoRouter.allowedModels: ["google/gemini-2.5-flash"]
  2. Send a message through the gateway
  3. Check OpenRouter Activity dashboard

  Expected

  - Only google/gemini-2.5-flash appears in the Activity log (single-model list = deterministic)

  Actual

  - Confirmed — only the allowlisted model was selected

  Evidence

  - Failing test/log before + passing after — 3 new unit tests in pi-embedded-runner-extraparams.test.ts, all passing

 ### Human Verification

  - Verified scenarios: allowedModels set with one model (deterministic), allowedModels absent (no plugin injected), allowedModels: [] empty array (no plugin injected)
  - Edge cases checked: Empty array treated same as absent — no plugins key injected. Existing provider routing param unaffected.
  - What you did not verify: Multi-model list behavior beyond unit test level (OpenRouter's internal selection logic is a black box); behavior with streaming edge cases at high concurrency.

   ### Compatibility / Migration

  - Backward compatible? Yes
  - Config/env changes? No
  - Migration needed? No

  ### Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

  - How to disable/revert quickly: Remove autoRouter.allowedModels from config — feature is opt-in, no effect when absent
  - Files/config to restore: extensions/openrouter/index.ts (revert injectAutoRouterPlugin addition and wrapStreamFn update)
  - Known bad symptoms: If plugins injection causes unexpected model selection, check OpenRouter Activity dashboard — the allowed_models field will be
  visible in the request log

  ### Risks and Mitigations

  - Risk: OpenRouter silently ignores plugins field on non-auto models, leading to no error but also no constraint.
  - Mitigation: Feature is gated on autoRouter.allowedModels being present — only applies when explicitly configured. Users setting this on a non-auto
  model is a config error, not a safety risk.

target.title: feat(agents): expose estimated context budget status
target.body:
## Summary

Expose OpenClaw's own pre-prompt context budget estimate on session/status surfaces when fresh provider token usage is unavailable.

- capture a typed `contextBudgetStatus` snapshot from the pre-prompt estimate
- persist that snapshot on `SessionEntry`
- expose a sanitized budget projection on gateway session rows
- let `/status` show estimated context usage only when fresh provider usage is unavailable or absent
- keep positive fresh `totalTokens` authoritative
- prevent preserved-runtime heartbeat runs from replacing the main session's budget snapshot
- reserve the new top-level session field from plugin session-entry slot mirroring

This is reporting-only. It does not change compaction thresholds, pruning, prompt-building, model routing, or payload policy.

Refs #80594, #54996, #77992, #84490, #83177, #43009, #83526, #8635.

## Verification

- `git diff --check refs/remotes/openclaw/main...HEAD`
- `.\node_modules\.bin\oxfmt.CMD --check src/gateway/session-utils.types.ts src/gateway/session-utils.ts src/gateway/session-utils.test.ts`
- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_FS_MODULE_CACHE_PATH=.artifacts/vitest-cache/budget2-gateway-row node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/session-utils.test.ts -t "session rows expose estimated context budget without transcript path" --reporter=verbose`
- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_FS_MODULE_CACHE_PATH=.artifacts/vitest-cache/budget2-session-store-rerun node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/command/session-store.test.ts -t "heartbeat bleed fix" --reporter=verbose`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/budget2-core-test.tsbuildinfo`
- Gateway row proof command with `node --import tsx .artifacts/pr84830-gateway-row-proof.mjs`, where the temporary script built a row containing an internal `contextBudgetStatus.sessionFile` and printed the public row projection below.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Sessions whose model/provider path cannot return fresh context usage can expose OpenClaw's own pre-prompt estimate instead of showing only unknown/zero context usage, heartbeat/preserved-runtime runs cannot overwrite the main session's budget snapshot, and gateway session rows expose the estimate without leaking the internal transcript path.

Real environment tested: Windows isolated worktree on PR head `16e3d343cd`, with local dependencies installed.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: `git diff --check refs/remotes/openclaw/main...HEAD`; `.\node_modules\.bin\oxfmt.CMD --check src/gateway/session-utils.types.ts src/gateway/session-utils.ts src/gateway/session-utils.test.ts`; focused Vitest for the gateway-row projection; focused Vitest for the heartbeat bleed guard; `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/budget2-core-test.tsbuildinfo`; and the gateway-row proof command described above.

Evidence after fix: Whitespace, formatting, and core test typecheck passed. The gateway-row regression passed in `gateway-core`, `gateway-server`, and `gateway-client`, proving the public row keeps estimated context budget fields while omitting `sessionFile`. The heartbeat regression passed in `agents-core` and `agents-support`, proving preserved-runtime heartbeat updates keep the existing Anthropic model/window and existing Anthropic budget snapshot instead of replacing it with the heartbeat Ollama snapshot.

Observed result after fix: Gateway session-row output from a row whose internal snapshot included `/redacted/openclaw/agents/main/sessions/session-1.jsonl` was:

```json
{
  "schemaVersion": 1,
  "source": "pre-prompt-estimate",
  "updatedAt": 2,
  "provider": "anthropic",
  "model": "claude-sonnet-4.6",
  "route": "compact_then_truncate",
  "shouldCompact": true,
  "estimatedPromptTokens": 640000,
  "contextTokenBudget": 200000,
  "promptBudgetBeforeReserve": 180000,
  "reserveTokens": 20000,
  "effectiveReserveTokens": 20000,
  "remainingPromptBudgetTokens": 0,
  "overflowTokens": 460000,
  "toolResultReducibleChars": 12000,
  "messageCount": 42,
  "unwindowedMessageCount": 39,
  "sessionId": "session-1"
}
```

The printed public row contains the estimated context-budget values and does not contain `sessionFile`.

What was not tested: Full Vitest files, broad `pnpm check:changed`, and a live provider-backed `/status` run were not rerun locally.

target.title: fix(ui): add agent selector to dreaming tab
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: the Control UI Dreaming tab had no agent selector, so users could not switch Dreaming context between agent workspaces.
- Why it matters: Dreaming artifacts (`DREAMS.md`, short-term recall, schedules, memory state) are agent-specific, so the tab was effectively stuck to the current session context.
- What changed: added an agent selector to the Dreaming tab chrome and wired it to the same preferred-session-per-agent logic used by chat session controls.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no backend dreaming behavior, routing behavior, or storage behavior was changed.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #63558
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Dreaming tab lacked an agent selector, preventing users from switching agent contexts there.
- Real environment tested: local checkout of `openclaw/openclaw` on Linux.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `cd ui && pnpm vitest run src/ui/views/dreaming.test.ts --reporter=verbose`
  - `pnpm tsgo:core`
  - `pnpm lint:core`
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):
```text
✓ src/ui/views/dreaming.test.ts > dreaming view > renders an agent selector and switches agent contexts
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       16 passed (16)
```
- Observed result after fix: the Dreaming tab now renders an agent selector when multiple agents are available, preselects the active agent, and triggers agent-context switching through the same preferred-session resolution used elsewhere.
- What was not tested: end-to-end browser interaction against a live multi-agent gateway.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Dreaming used its own tab chrome and never reused the shared agent-selection/session-switching logic used in chat.
- Missing detection / guardrail: Dreaming view tests did not assert presence of an agent selector for multi-agent setups.
- Contributing context (if known): the rest of Control UI already assumed agent-specific contexts for many views, so Dreaming lagged behind that UX pattern.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `ui/src/ui/views/dreaming.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: when multiple agents are available, Dreaming shows an agent selector, reflects the active agent, and calls back when the selection changes.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the missing feature is entirely in the Dreaming tab view/chrome layer.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): branch adds `renders an agent selector and switches agent contexts`.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

The Control UI Dreaming tab now shows an agent selector when multiple agent contexts are available, so users can switch Dreaming context directly from the tab.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: local repo checkout
- Area: Control UI / Dreaming tab

### Steps

1. Render Dreaming with multiple available agent options.
2. Verify an agent selector is present.
3. Verify the current agent is selected.
4. Change the selection and verify the callback fires with the target agent id.

### Expected

- Dreaming shows the current agent and allows agent switching.

### Actual

- Dreaming view test passes with the new selector behavior.
- `tsgo:core` passes.
- `lint:core` passes.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified the selector appears in the Dreaming tab chrome.
- Verified the selected agent is reflected in the UI.
- Verified agent changes route through preferred-session-per-agent logic.
- Verified view test, `tsgo:core`, and `lint:core` all pass.

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: the selector could route users to an unexpected session for an agent.
  - Mitigation: it uses the same preferred-session-per-agent resolution logic already used in chat controls.
- Risk: single-agent setups could get cluttered.
  - Mitigation: the selector renders only when more than one agent option is available.



---

## Real behavior proof

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway-methods.config.ts src/gateway/server-methods/doctor.test.ts

 RUN  v4.1.5 /tmp/pincer-openclaw

 ✓  gateway-methods  src/gateway/server-methods/doctor.test.ts (27 tests) 89ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
       Tests  27 passed (27)
    Start at  00:25:12
    Duration  470ms
```

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Selecting an agent in the Dreaming tab called `switchChatSession()` but `loadDreamingStatus` and `loadDreamDiary` still sent `{}` to `doctor.memory.*`, showing the default agent's data regardless of selection. The tab could display DREAMS.md and memory state for the wrong agent.
**Real environment tested:** Node.js v24.14.0, vitest v4.1.5, Linux.
**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway-methods.config.ts src/gateway/server-methods/doctor.test.ts`
**After-fix evidence:** 27/27 gateway method tests pass. TypeScript `tsc --noEmit` reports no errors across core and extensions configs.
**Observed result after fix:** `onSelectAgent` sets `selectedAgentId` on state, immediately reloads `dreamingStatus` and `dreamDiary` scoped to the selected agent. Gateway handlers accept optional `agentId` param with backward-compatible fallback to `resolveDefaultAgentId` when omitted.
**What was not tested:** Live multi-agent Control UI browser session; gateway handler routing is verified at the unit level through the existing 27 gateway method tests.

target.title: feat(plugins): add trust tier classification for plugin security model
target.body:
## Summary

- Formalizes the implicit native vs bundle trust boundary into explicit **trust tiers** (`content` | `sandboxed` | `native`)
- Adds `PluginTrustTier` type, `resolveTrustTier()` pure function, and wires it into the plugin loader
- Surfaces trust tier in `plugins list --verbose` and `plugins info` CLI output
- Reserves `sandboxed` tier for future ACP-backed tool execution
- No behavior change — pure metadata addition + documentation

## Motivation

Today OpenClaw has an implicit trust boundary: native plugins get full in-process `api.runtime` access, bundles get content-only access. This PR makes that boundary explicit in the type system, CLI output, and documentation — creating the foundation for safely expanding bundle capabilities (especially the future sandboxed tier).

## Changes

| File | What |
|------|------|
| `src/plugins/types.ts` | `PluginTrustTier` type union |
| `src/plugins/trust.ts` | `resolveTrustTier()` — format → tier mapping |
| `src/plugins/registry.ts` | `trustTier` field on `PluginRecord` |
| `src/plugins/loader.ts` | Wire `resolveTrustTier()` at both `createPluginRecord` call sites |
| `src/cli/plugins-cli.ts` | Show trust tier in `plugins info` + verbose list |
| `src/plugins/trust.test.ts` | 17 unit tests — all format × origin combos |
| `docs/plugins/trust-tiers.md` | Design doc with tier definitions, assignment rules, security rationale |
| `docs/plugins/bundles.md` | Cross-reference to trust tiers doc |
| `src/commands/onboarding/plugin-install.test.ts` | Add required `trustTier` to mock record |

## Test plan

- [x] `tsc --noEmit` passes (non-optional `trustTier` caught everywhere)
- [x] 17/17 new unit tests pass (all format × origin × bundleFormat combos)
- [x] Existing test suite unaffected (no regressions)
- [ ] Manual: `openclaw plugins list --verbose` shows trust tier
- [ ] Manual: `openclaw plugins info <bundle-id>` shows "Trust tier: content"
- [ ] Manual: `openclaw plugins info <native-id>` shows "Trust tier: native"

[AI-assisted]

## Summary

- Adds `detectResourceProfile()` utility (`low`/`standard`/`high`) based on total RAM and CPU architecture, providing a single detection point for constrained-device tuning
- Adapts FFmpeg buffer sizes, image resize grid dimensions, quality compression steps, embedding concurrency, and plugin cache limits on low-profile (ARM/<2GB) devices
- Adds systemd `MemoryHigh`/`MemoryMax` cgroup limits for low-profile daemon installs so the gateway stays within safe bounds on Raspberry Pi
- Lowers LanceDB vector search limits on <2GB devices in the `memory-lancedb` extension (uses `node:os` directly since extensions are standalone packages)
- Adds Raspberry Pi platform documentation (`docs/platforms/raspberry-pi.md`)

### Commits

1. `infra: add platform resource profile detection for ARM/Pi tuning` — foundation module with `detectResourceProfile()` and tests
2. `feat: adaptive resource limits for ARM/low-memory devices` — consumers across media pipeline, memory, plugins, and docs

- `src/infra/platform-profile.ts` (new) — profile detection logic

target.title: feat: add /trust and /untrust commands for temporary exec security elevation
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Existing exec approval workflows are binary and permanent.  Users get inundated with confirmation requests and likely disable approvals entirely, defeating the security model.
- Why it matters: This feature increases usable security surface by introducing temporary time-bound escalation, a pattern common in sudo timeouts and auth tokens. Users can grant temporary full access as needed instead of permanently disabling protections.
- What changed: Added `/trust [minutes]` and `/untrust` slash commands with in-memory-only state, auto-expiry, anti-stacking, and a single enforcement point for all surfaces.  Doesn't touch the security model, just acts as a wrapper for existing exec_security setting.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No changes to existing `/exec` behavior, config schema, persistence layer, or approval flows. No per-surface wiring — uses existing command registry

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #47695

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- New `/trust [minutes]` command: temporarily sets `exec.security=full` for the current session (1-480 minutes, default 15)
- New `/untrust` command: immediately revokes temporary trust
- Anti-stacking: `/trust` while already trusted is refused with remaining time shown
- Trust is in-memory only — lost on process restart (fail-closed by design)
- Available on all surfaces (CLI, TUI, Discord, Telegram, Slack, WhatsApp, web) via existing command registry

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `Yes` — temporary exec escalation to `security=full`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `Yes` — two new slash commands that affect exec security resolution
- Data access scope changed? `No`

Risk + mitigation:
- **Risk**: Unauthorized user could escalate exec permissions
  - **Mitigation**: Gated by existing `rejectUnauthorizedCommand` — same auth check as `/exec`
- **Risk**: Trust could persist beyond intended window
  - **Mitigation**: In-memory only (`Map` with `setTimeout`), never written to disk, lost on restart (fail-closed). Timer uses `.unref()` matching codebase convention
- **Risk**: Trust could leak across sessions
  - **Mitigation**: Keyed by `sessionKey`, same scoping as all other session state
- **Risk**: Anti-stacking bypass via `/untrust` then `/trust`
  - **Mitigation**: This is intentional and strictly less powerful than existing `/exec security=full` which is permanent. Cycling requires explicit authorized user action each time
- **Design**: Trust sits in the exec resolution chain at `inline directive > trust > session entry`, matching existing `/exec` precedence. Does not bypass agent-level exec ceilings in `exec-approvals.json`

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Fedora Linux
- Runtime/container: OpenClaw v3.14 from keylimesoda/openclaw branch
- Model/provider: Any
- Integration/channel (if any): All surfaces (tested CLI)
- Relevant config (redacted): Default exec config

### Steps

1. Start a session
2. Send `/trust 15`
3. Observe trust grant message with duration
4. Send `/trust 30` — observe anti-stacking refusal
5. Send `/untrust` — observe revocation
6. Send `/trust 5` — observe re-grant works after untrust
7. Wait for expiry — observe trust auto-revokes

### Expected

- Trust granted with clear feedback, refused when already active, revocable, auto-expires

### Actual

- All behaviors match expected. All user-facing strings reviewed and refined

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

7/7 unit tests pass covering: default window, anti-stacking, auto-expiry, /untrust revocation, bounds validation, unauthorized rejection, numerical input scrubbing (0, -5, 15.5, 481, abc, 1e5).

Build (`pnpm build`), typecheck (`pnpm tsgo`), and format all pass.

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: I verified all trust/untrust command paths tested on OpenClaw v3.14, Fedora Linux. Trust grant, anti-stacking refusal, untrust revocation, re-trust after untrust, and input bounds checking.  All user-facing strings reviewed and updated across multiple iterations
- Edge cases checked: Zero minutes, negative minutes, decimal minutes, scientific notation, above-max minutes, untrust with no active window, untrust with extra args
- What you did **not** verify: Cross-surface testing on Discord/Telegram/Slack native commands (verified via command registry integration which serves all surfaces identically). Did not test 480-minute real-time expiry.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes` — no existing behavior changed
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: Designed to have as little impact to existing codebase as possible.  Single `git revert` of this commit removes the entire feature. Alternatively, delete 2 new files and revert 3 surgical edits (import line, 2 handler entries, 1 resolution chain call)
- Files/config to restore: `commands-registry.data.ts`, `commands-core.ts`, `get-reply-directives.ts` (remove added lines)
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: Trust persisting beyond expected window (check timer cleanup), trust applying to wrong session (check sessionKey scoping), process hanging on exit (check `.unref()` on timer)

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Module-level `Map` state grows unbounded
  - Mitigation: Entries auto-expire via `setTimeout` and are bounded by authorized sessions (not user-controllable). Each entry is one Map key + timer
- Risk: Timer keeps Node process alive
  - Mitigation: `.unref()` called on timer, matching 15+ codebase precedents for background timers

target.title: fix(slack): instrument DM inbound pipeline with verbose drop logging
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds `logVerbose` trace points at every drop gate in the Slack DM inbound pipeline so intermittent message losses become diagnosable when verbose mode is enabled
- Fixes `appMentionRetryKeys` cleanup in `onFlush` to cover all batched entries on successful dispatch (not just the last entry), preventing stale map growth

## Root cause

DM messages were disappearing without any trace at default log levels. All drop decisions (`dedup drop`, `prepared=null`, `empty content`, `app_mention race`) were either silent or only logged via `logVerbose` from inside `prepare.ts`, making it impossible to distinguish authorization failures from content resolution failures from debounce issues.

## Changes

**`extensions/slack/src/monitor/message-handler.ts`**
- Import `logVerbose` / `shouldLogVerbose` from globals
- `slack inbound DM:` trace on every DM event entering the handler (channel, ts, source, user, subtype)
- `slack inbound: dedup drop` when `markMessageSeen` returns true
- `slack inbound: drop unprepared` in `onFlush` when `prepareSlackMessage` returns null; includes entry count and source so batched drops are attributable
- `slack inbound: app_mention race drop` when a message event is suppressed because app_mention already dispatched
- Fix: clean up `appMentionRetryKeys` for **all** entries in a batched flush on successful dispatch; the `!prepared` path leaves retry keys alive intentionally so in-flight app_mention events can retry once

**`extensions/slack/src/monitor/message-handler/prepare.ts`**
- `slack inbound: drop empty content` when `resolveSlackMessageContent` returns null; includes `isDirectMessage`, `text_len`, and `files` count for diagnosis

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm test -- message-handler` — 7 tests pass (3 new: DM trace logged, dedup drop logged, no log when verbose off)
- [x] `pnpm test -- message-handler.app-mention-race` — 6 tests pass (2 new: onFlush unprepared drop logged, no dispatch for null prepared batch)
- [x] `pnpm test -- extensions/slack` — 386 tests pass (all pre-existing pass)
- [x] `pnpm tsgo` on changed files — no new errors
- [x] `pnpm format:fix` applied

## To test the fix in production

Enable verbose logging and reproduce a lost DM. The new trace points will show:
1. Whether the event reached the handler at all
2. Whether it was deduplicated
3. Whether `prepareSlackMessage` dropped it (and why via existing `logVerbose` inside prepare)
4. Whether content was empty

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: Telegram: add reaction notification wake policy
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Telegram reaction events were queued as passive context, so reaction-only inputs did not wake the routed agent session.
- Why it matters: operators may want selected notification events to wake a routed session, but making every accepted reaction wake by default is a compatibility change.
- What changed: accepted Telegram reaction events now include stable `reaction_key` values for standard and custom emoji and route through shared `notificationWake.reactions` policy.
- Compatibility boundary: reaction events stay queue-only by default; set `channels.telegram.notificationWake.reactions: "wake"` to request immediate routed heartbeat wakes.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #47677
- Related: #83611
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: accepted Telegram reaction-only events now enqueue stable reaction-key system events and wake the routed session only when the resolved shared notification wake policy is `wake`. Default policy remains `queue`, preserving existing reaction notification compatibility.
- Real environment tested: Redacted OpenClaw development checkout on PR head `1bde45dcc7`, plus the earlier live Telegram Bot API canary for bot identity/message reachability. No secrets, usernames, chat titles, local paths, or raw IDs are included here.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/notification-system-events.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.test.ts`
  - `pnpm config:channels:check`
  - `pnpm config:docs:check`
  - `pnpm tsgo:prod`
  - `pnpm test:contracts:plugins`
  - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 <touched files>`
  - `git diff --check`
- Evidence after fix: Redacted terminal verification from PR head:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/notification-system-events.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.test.ts
Test Files  3 passed (3)
Tests  159 passed (159)

pnpm config:channels:check
passed

pnpm config:docs:check
OK docs/.generated/config-baseline.sha256

pnpm tsgo:prod
passed

pnpm test:contracts:plugins
Test Files  62 passed (62)
Tests  857 passed (857)

pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 <touched files>
All matched files use the correct format.

git diff --check
passed
```

Supplemental earlier live canary proof, redacted: Telegram `getMe` succeeded and a canary message posted to the configured target. This proves Bot API reachability, but not a production reaction update wake.
- Observed result after fix: The shared helper queues notification system events by default, skips enqueue for `off`, wakes only for resolved `wake`, preserves dedupe no-wake behavior, and resolves account policy before channel, agent, channel defaults, and global defaults. Telegram handler tests prove queue-by-default, opt-in wake, custom emoji stable keys, trust downgrade, authorization/routing preservation, and no wake when enqueue deduplicates.
- What was not tested: I did not capture a live Telegram Desktop reaction update waking a production routed session after this policy patch. The PR still has the `mantis: telegram-visible-proof` label so maintainer/Mantis proof can cover that external surface.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): current main enqueues accepted Telegram reaction system events and returns without requesting a heartbeat wake.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Telegram `message_reaction` handling enqueued system events and then returned without a policy-controlled wake path.
- Missing detection / guardrail: existing tests asserted enqueue behavior but did not assert notification wake policy, default queue compatibility, or deduped no-wake behavior.
- Contributing context (if known): Telegram custom emoji reactions were not represented in the event text because only standard emoji reactions were collected.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Contract test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target tests/files: `src/infra/notification-system-events.test.ts`, `extensions/telegram/src/bot.test.ts`, `extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.test.ts`, `pnpm test:contracts:plugins`
- Scenario the test should lock in: accepted standard/custom emoji reactions enqueue stable reaction-key events, stay queue-only by default, wake only under `notificationWake.reactions: "wake"`, and do not wake when enqueue deduplicates.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the shared helper owns policy precedence and heartbeat behavior; the Telegram handler harness owns reaction authorization/routing/enqueue behavior.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Telegram reaction notifications that pass existing access controls enqueue system events by default. Operators can opt into immediate routed wakes with `channels.telegram.notificationWake.reactions: "wake"`. System event text includes `reaction_key=emoji:<emoji>` or `reaction_key=custom_emoji:<id>` so preference/prompt text can assign meaning to specific reactions without adding per-emoji channel schema.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Default:
[Telegram reaction] -> [system event queued] -> [processed on next scheduled/explicit wake]

Opt-in wake:
[Telegram reaction] -> [notificationWake.reactions = wake] -> [system event queued] -> [heartbeat wake] -> [agent sees reaction event]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: redacted local development environment
- Runtime/container: local source checkout
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Telegram handler unit harness and shared notification system-event helper
- Relevant config (redacted): `channels.telegram.reactionNotifications: "all"`, `dmPolicy: "open"`, `allowFrom: ["*"]`, and opt-in wake case with `notificationWake.reactions: "wake"`

### Steps

1. Run shared notification wake helper tests plus focused Telegram reaction and dispatch tests.
2. Run generated config metadata and docs checks.
3. Run production typecheck and plugin contract tests.
4. Run format and whitespace checks.

### Expected

- Default reaction notifications enqueue without wake.
- `notificationWake.reactions: "wake"` wakes the routed session after enqueue.
- `notificationWake.reactions: "off"` skips notification enqueue.
- Deduped notification events do not wake.
- Typecheck, generated config checks, plugin contracts, format, and whitespace checks pass.

### Actual

- Passed locally on PR head `1bde45dcc7`.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: queue-by-default reaction notification, explicit wake policy, off policy, account policy precedence, dedupe no-wake, standard emoji stable key, custom emoji stable key, existing authorization/routing cases.
- Edge cases checked: reaction removals ignored, `own`/`all`/`off` reaction modes, bot-user reactions ignored, forum/general topic routing remains unchanged.
- What you did **not** verify: live Telegram Desktop user reaction update waking a production routed session.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? Yes, optional `notificationWake.reactions` policy surface
- Migration needed? No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: reaction notifications could start an agent turn unexpectedly.
  - Mitigation: default policy is `queue`; immediate wake requires explicit `notificationWake.reactions: "wake"` and remains gated by existing Telegram sender/access controls.
- Risk: shared policy precedence could surprise users with layered defaults.
  - Mitigation: tests cover account over channel over agent/global defaults, and docs state the channel-level setting.

target.title: fix(messages): add `unscheduledReminderNote` config to disable reminder guard note
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: The reminder guard appends "Note: I did not schedule a reminder in this turn, so this will not trigger automatically." to agent replies when it detects phrases like "I'll remember" or "I'll follow up". The regex frequently produces false positives on conversational language (e.g. "I'll remember that" meaning knowledge retention).
- Why it matters: Users see phantom text appended to agent replies with no way to opt out. The note is indistinguishable from agent-authored content.
- What changed: Added `messages.unscheduledReminderNote` (boolean, default `true`) to gate `appendUnscheduledReminderNote`. Set `false` to disable. Added 25 unit tests covering detection, appending, and config flag gating.
- What did NOT change: The reminder guard logic itself (`hasUnbackedReminderCommitment`, regex patterns, cron-store check). Default behavior is preserved.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #30787

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

New config option:
```json5
{ messages: { unscheduledReminderNote: false } }
```
When set to `false`, the system no longer appends the "Note: I did not schedule a reminder..." text to agent replies. Default (`true` / omitted) preserves current behavior.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`No`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`No`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`)
- Data access scope changed? (`No`)

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node 22, local dev
- Model/provider: N/A (text post-processing, not model-dependent)
- Integration/channel: Telegram (where the phantom text was observed)
- Relevant config: `messages.unscheduledReminderNote: false`

### Steps

1. Send an agent reply containing "I'll remember that" with no cron job created
2. With default config: observe the "Note: I did not schedule..." appended to delivery
3. With `messages.unscheduledReminderNote: false`: observe clean delivery without appendage

### Expected

- Default: note appended (backward compatible)
- `false`: no note appended

### Actual

- Matches expected in both cases

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- 25 unit tests added covering: commitment detection (7 positive, 5 negative), note appending (7 cases), and config flag gating (5 cases)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: Production false positive observed twice in a live Telegram session — agent said "I'll remember" (knowledge retention), guard appended phantom note to delivery. Root cause traced in source to `REMINDER_COMMITMENT_PATTERNS` regex matching bare "remember". Unit tests confirm config flag gates the behavior correctly for all three states (undefined/true/false).
- Edge cases checked: Empty text, whitespace-only text, error payloads, media-only payloads, multiple payloads, already-appended note, third-person references.
- What you did **not** verify: Full E2E with a running gateway instance with the config flag set. The integration point in `agent-runner.ts` is a single-line conditional addition.
## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`) — default `true` preserves existing behavior
- Config/env changes? (`Yes`) — new optional key `messages.unscheduledReminderNote`
- Migration needed? (`No`)

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert: Remove `messages.unscheduledReminderNote` from config (reverts to default `true`)
- Files/config to restore: None needed, the flag is purely additive
- Known bad symptoms: None — worst case is the note not appearing when it should (operator explicitly opted out)

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Operator disables the note and then forgets they made unbacked reminder promises to users.
  - Mitigation: This is the operator's choice. The guard logic still runs internally; only the visible note is suppressed.

target.title: Dockerfile: simplify multi-stage build
target.body:
- Reduce from 5 stages to 2 (build + runtime)
- Remove retry loops for Bun and Corepack installs
- Hardcode bookworm-slim runtime (removes variant switching)
- Inline extension copy (removes ext-deps stage)
- Remove SHA256 digest pinning for simpler maintenance
- Preserve all original comments, variable names, and optional features

## Summary

- Problem: The Dockerfile uses 5 build stages, retry loops, variant switching via ARGs, and SHA256 digest pinning, making it difficult to read, maintain, and debug for contributors and self-hosters.
- Why it matters: A simpler Dockerfile lowers the barrier for contributions, speeds up local iteration, and reduces the surface area for build failures caused by stage interdependencies.
- What changed: Consolidated 5 stages into 2 (build + runtime), removed retry loops for Bun/Corepack installs, hardcoded bookworm-slim as the runtime base, replaced the ext-deps stage with a direct COPY, and removed SHA256 digest pinning in favor of standard image tags.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): All variable names (`OPENCLAW_DOCKER_APT_PACKAGES`, `OPENCLAW_INSTALL_BROWSER`, `OPENCLAW_INSTALL_DOCKER_CLI`, `OPENCLAW_DOCKER_GPG_FINGERPRINT`), optional feature blocks (browser, Docker CLI, extra apt packages), security hardening (non-root user, healthcheck), build steps (`pnpm install`, a2ui bundle, build:docker, ui:build, prune), and the runtime CMD are unchanged.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [x] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [x] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- The `OPENCLAW_VARIANT` build arg is removed; the runtime image is always based on `node:24-bookworm-slim`. Users who explicitly built with `OPENCLAW_VARIANT=default` (full bookworm) will need to adjust or use the slim image.
- The `OPENCLAW_EXTENSIONS` build arg and selective extension filtering are removed; all extensions in the `extensions/` directory are now copied unconditionally.
- Image tags are no longer pinned to SHA256 digests; builds will pull the latest `node:24-bookworm` and `node:24-bookworm-slim` tags.
- Bun and Corepack installs no longer retry on transient failures; builds on flaky networks may need manual retries.
- APT layer caching uses `rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*` instead of named `--mount=type=cache` mounts.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No` - same upstream sources (bun.sh, npm registry, apt repos, Docker GPG key)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux (Arch Linux Testing)
- Runtime/container: Docker 29.3.0 with BuildKit/buildx v0.32.1
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): Default build with no optional build args

### Steps

1. `DOCKER_BUILDKIT=1 docker build -t openclaw-simplified .`
2. `docker run --rm openclaw-simplified node -e "console.log('ok')"`
3. `docker run --rm openclaw-simplified node openclaw.mjs gateway --help`

### Expected

- Image builds successfully in 2 stages
- Container starts and runs Node.js commands
- Gateway help output is displayed

### Actual

- Image built successfully in 2 stages (`build` 15 steps + `stage-1` 16 steps) in ~192s
  - Final image: `openclaw-simplified:latest` - 4.08GB disk / 1.02GB content (sha256:a09af4b5f88c)
- Container starts and runs Node.js: `docker run --rm openclaw-simplified node -e "console.log('ok')"` → `ok`
- Gateway help output displayed: `docker run --rm openclaw-simplified node openclaw.mjs gateway --help` → full help text with all subcommands rendered correctly
- OpenClaw version: `2026.3.14 (unknown)`
- Runtime user: `node` (non-root, uid 1000)

## Evidence

- [x] Trace/log snippets [openclaw-build.txt](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/26009537/openclaw-build.txt)

Full build + verification log attached: [openclaw-build.log](cci:7://file:///home/szoltysek/openclaw-build.log:0:0-0:0) (228 lines)
Contains: BuildKit build output (39 steps, all succeeded), node smoke test (`ok`), `gateway --help` full output, and `docker images` size info.

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: Built the image locally with `DOCKER_BUILDKIT=1 docker build -t openclaw-simplified .`; ran `node -e "console.log('ok')"` in the container; ran `node openclaw.mjs gateway --help` and confirmed full help output; verified image size with `docker images`.
- Edge cases checked: Optional build args (`OPENCLAW_DOCKER_APT_PACKAGES`, `OPENCLAW_INSTALL_BROWSER`, `OPENCLAW_INSTALL_DOCKER_CLI`) are still present and conditionally executed (confirmed by build log showing `if [ -n "" ]` no-op steps); A2UI bundle fallback stub logic is preserved.
- What you did **not** verify: Cross-architecture builds (ARM/QEMU); builds with optional args enabled (`OPENCLAW_INSTALL_BROWSER=1`, `OPENCLAW_INSTALL_DOCKER_CLI=1`); performance comparison against the original 5-stage Dockerfile.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `No` - the `OPENCLAW_VARIANT` and `OPENCLAW_EXTENSIONS` build args are removed.
- Config/env changes? `Yes` - users relying on `OPENCLAW_VARIANT=default` (full bookworm) or selective `OPENCLAW_EXTENSIONS` filtering need to adjust.
- Migration needed? `Yes`
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:
  1. If you used `--build-arg OPENCLAW_VARIANT=default`, remove that arg. The runtime is now always bookworm-slim. If you need packages only in full bookworm, add them via `--build-arg OPENCLAW_DOCKER_APT_PACKAGES="..."`.
  2. If you used `--build-arg OPENCLAW_EXTENSIONS="ext1 ext2"`, remove that arg. All extensions are now copied. To exclude extensions, remove them from the `extensions/` directory before building.
  3. If you pinned builds to specific SHA256 digests, note that builds now track the `node:24-bookworm` / `node:24-bookworm-slim` rolling tags.

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert the single `Dockerfile` to the previous commit: `git revert <commit-sha>`
- Files/config to restore: `Dockerfile` (single file change)
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for:
  - `curl: (22) The requested URL returned error: 5xx` during Bun install (no retry loop - re-run the build)
  - Missing extensions at runtime if the `extensions/` directory was not fully populated at build time
  - Larger image size if unneeded extensions are included (previously filtered by OPENCLAW_EXTENSIONS)

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Bun or Corepack install fails on transient network errors without retry loops.
  - Mitigation: Docker/Buildx layer caching means re-running the build retries only the failed layer. CI systems typically have their own retry mechanisms. The retry loops can be re-added if this proves to be a recurring issue in CI.

- Risk: Removing SHA256 digest pinning means builds are no longer fully reproducible - the `node:24-bookworm` tag can move.
  - Mitigation: For production/release builds, users can pin digests in their own `docker-compose.yml` or CI pipeline. The simplification benefits outweigh the reproducibility trade-off for the majority of users.

- Risk: Removing `OPENCLAW_EXTENSIONS` filtering may increase image size if many extensions exist.
  - Mitigation: Extensions are typically small (package.json + JS files). The ext-deps stage only saved space for users with many unused extensions. Users needing minimal images can prune `extensions/` before building.

- Risk: Removing the full bookworm variant may break users who depend on packages only available in the non-slim image.
  - Mitigation: The `OPENCLAW_DOCKER_APT_PACKAGES` build arg remains available to install any additional packages. The slim image + explicit apt installs is the more common Docker pattern.

target.title: fix(reminder-guard): reduce false positives on memory-related statements
target.body:
## Problem

The reminder guard's `REMINDER_COMMITMENT_PATTERNS` regex includes bare `remember` which matches memory/knowledge retention statements like "I'll remember that" and "I'll remember your preference" — not just temporal reminder commitments. This causes a confusing disclaimer to appear in agent responses that have nothing to do with reminders.

**Example:** Agent responds with "I'll remember your preference!" → user sees:
> Note: I did not schedule a reminder in this turn, so this will not trigger automatically.

Additionally, the cron-based suppression checks for the existence of ANY cron job rather than whether a reminder was scheduled in the current turn, making the guard both too aggressive (false positives with no cron) and too lenient (completely suppressed with any unrelated cron).

## Changes

### 1. Narrow `REMINDER_COMMITMENT_PATTERNS`

Remove bare `remember` from the first pattern. Add a separate pattern for `remember to [verb]` which implies temporal reminder intent:

```js
// Before (too broad)
/(?:remember|remind|ping|follow up|...)/i

// After (precise)
/(?:remind|ping|follow up|...)/i,           // unambiguous reminder verbs
/remember\s+to\s+/i                          // only "remember to [verb]"
```

**True positives preserved:** "I'll remind you", "I'll follow up", "I'll remember to check on that"  
**False positives eliminated:** "I'll remember that", "I'll remember the specifics", "I'll remember your preference"

### 2. Add 33 unit tests

First test file for `agent-runner-reminder-guard`:
- 16 true positive cases (genuine reminder commitments)
- 8 false positive cases (memory/knowledge retention — previously incorrectly matched)
- 5 edge cases (empty strings, already-appended note, third-person mentions)
- 4 integration tests for `appendUnscheduledReminderNote()`

### Note on cron suppression scoping

The current `hasSessionRelatedCronJobs()` suppresses the guard when _any_ enabled cron job exists for the session, even unrelated ones (e.g. a weekly report). Ideally this should check whether a cron job was created in the _current turn_. This is a deeper change that may warrant a separate PR — flagged in #47586.

## Testing

```
✓ src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-reminder-guard.test.ts (33 tests) 3ms
Test Files  1 passed (1)
     Tests  33 passed (33)
```

Closes #47586

target.title: feat(telegram): bi-directional custom emoji reactions
target.body:
## Summary

Adds support for Telegram custom emoji reactions in both directions:

### Inbound — Receive custom emoji reactions
- `bot-handlers.ts`: Now detects `type: "custom_emoji"` reactions alongside standard emoji reactions
- Custom emoji reactions surface as system events with the `custom_emoji_id` in event text
- Context key format: `telegram:reaction:add:{chatId}:{msgId}:{userId}:custom_emoji:{customEmojiId}`
- Mixed updates (both emoji + custom emoji in one reaction update) are handled correctly

### Outbound — Send custom emoji reactions
- `reactMessageTelegram()` accepts optional `customEmojiId` parameter
- When `customEmojiId` is provided, sends `{type: "custom_emoji", custom_emoji_id: "..."}` instead of `{type: "emoji", emoji: "..."}`
- `customEmojiId` takes priority when both emoji and customEmojiId are provided
- `telegram-actions.ts`: The `react` action now accepts `customEmojiId` parameter

### Tests
- 3 new `send.test.ts` cases: custom emoji send, priority over standard emoji, remove with custom emoji
- 3 new `bot.test.ts` cases: custom emoji inbound event, mixed emoji+custom_emoji, removal (no event)

### Notes on Custom Emoji IDs
- Custom emoji reactions require **Telegram Premium** on the reacting user's account
- Custom emoji IDs are numeric strings (e.g., `"5368324170671202286"`)
- To find a custom emoji ID: receive a custom emoji reaction inbound (it will appear in the system event), or use the Telegram Bot API `getCustomEmojiStickers` method
- The bot itself does NOT need Premium to set custom emoji reactions, but the chat must allow them

### Quality
- `npx tsc --noEmit`: No new errors (pre-existing errors in device-pair/chrome-mcp unchanged)
- `npx vitest run extensions/telegram/src/send.test.ts`: 68/68 pass (3 new)
- `npm run build`: Succeeds

target.title: docker: fix docker-setup.sh .env handling
target.body:
## Problem

Two related issues with `docker-setup.sh` and `.env` handling:

### 1. `docker-setup.sh` does not source `.env`

Variables defined in `.env` (e.g. `OPENCLAW_DOCKER_APT_PACKAGES`, `OPENCLAW_INSTALL_BROWSER`) were silently ignored unless the caller manually ran `source .env` before invoking `docker-setup.sh`. This is a common footgun — the file exists and looks authoritative, but has no effect on the build.

### 2. `upsert_env()` strips quotes from whitespace-containing values

`upsert_env()` rewrites `.env` using bare `printf '%s=%s\n'`, which drops quotes. After a `docker-setup.sh` run, a value like:

```
OPENCLAW_DOCKER_APT_PACKAGES="jq ripgrep"
```

becomes:

```
OPENCLAW_DOCKER_APT_PACKAGES=jq ripgrep
```

On the next invocation, `source .env` fails with `command not found: ripgrep`, silently breaking multi-package build args.

## Fix

- **Source `.env` at startup** using `set -a / source / set +a` so all `.env` variables are exported before the script proceeds.
- **Add `_write_kv()` helper** in `upsert_env()` that wraps values containing spaces or tabs in double quotes when writing back to `.env`.

## Testing

```bash
echo 'OPENCLAW_DOCKER_APT_PACKAGES="jq ripgrep"' >> .env
echo 'OPENCLAW_INSTALL_BROWSER=1' >> .env
./docker-setup.sh
grep -E 'APT_PACKAGES|INSTALL_BROWSER' .env
# Expected: values preserved with quotes intact
```

target.title: feat(doctor): add --check-config for deep config validation
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds `openclaw doctor --check-config` for deep config validation that goes beyond schema parsing
- Two-layer approach: schema + plugin validation first, then subsystem dry-run checks (model resolution, model catalog, fallback chain, TTS provider, channel config)
- Catches silent config degradation where the gateway starts and looks healthy but features like TTS or model fallbacks are quietly broken
- Non-zero exit code on any failure for CI pipeline gating

Closes #45552
Closes #20909

## Motivation

Currently `openclaw doctor` validates auth profiles, gateway health, sandbox images, and more — but does not exercise config-dependent initialization paths. This means a user can upgrade OpenClaw, have the gateway start cleanly, and only discover that TTS stopped working (or model fallbacks broke) when they try to use the feature.

Real-world example from #45540: the `ANTHROPIC_MODEL_ALIASES` TDZ bug caused silent config degradation — gateway started, channels connected, cron fired normally — but TTS initialization was broken. Debugging took ~2 hours. `doctor --check-config` would have caught it immediately.

## What it checks

| Check | Category | Catches |
|-------|----------|---------|
| Config schema + plugins | Schema | Typos, structural errors, unknown keys, plugin config |
| Primary model resolution | Model | Broken model refs, unresolvable aliases |
| Model catalog lookup | Model | Models not in catalog (will fail at runtime) |
| Fallback model chain | Model | Unparseable fallback refs in global defaults and per-agent overrides |
| TTS provider availability | TTS | Missing API keys, disabled edge provider, silent fallback |
| Channel config | Channels | Enabled channels listing, all-disabled detection |

## Example output

```
✅ Config schema
✅ Primary model — anthropic/claude-opus-4-6
✅ Model catalog
✅ Model fallbacks — 2 fallbacks validated
⚠️  TTS provider — configured provider "openai" missing API key — will fall back to "edge"
✅ Channel: telegram — enabled
```

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm build` passes
- [x] `pnpm check` (lint + format) passes
- [x] `pnpm test -- src/commands/doctor-check-config.test.ts` — 7 tests pass
- [ ] Manual test: `openclaw doctor --check-config` on a live config
- [ ] Manual test: verify non-zero exit code when config has failures

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed:
`openclaw doctor --check-config` should load from the real CLI path and report config validation results instead of failing during TypeScript compilation.

Real environment tested:
Local macOS checkout of OpenClaw on branch `pr-47302-ci-fix`, Node 24.10.0, pnpm 11.1.0.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
`pnpm tsgo:core`
`pnpm openclaw doctor --check-config`

Evidence after fix:
`pnpm tsgo:core` completed with `EXIT:0` after running `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core.tsbuildinfo`.
The real CLI command built the stale dist path, reached the new check-config command, and printed `Config file — config file not found` instead of crashing on the previous `TTS_PROVIDERS` import/type error.

Observed result after fix:
TypeScript production checking passes, and the real CLI reaches `doctor --check-config` runtime behavior. In a checkout without a configured OpenClaw config file, it fails closed with exit code 1 and a clear config-file diagnostic.

What was not tested:
A live configured OpenClaw installation with valid model/TTS credentials was not exercised locally; this pass verifies the compile-time regression and real CLI command path.

## Design decisions

- **Dynamic import** of the new module from `register.maintenance.ts` to avoid adding startup weight for the standard `doctor` path
- **Structured results** (`CheckConfigResult[]`) rather than direct console output, making it testable and extensible for JSON output later
- **Two-layer approach** as suggested in the issue discussion: fast schema check first, then subsystem dry-run
- **Every check always produces output** — no silent skips when a subsystem cannot be validated

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: fix(platform): extend startup optimization hints to support macOS (darwin)
target.body:
## Summary

Extend startup optimization hints to macOS (darwin) platform, especially for Apple Silicon (ARM64) and low-memory environments.

## Problem

Previously, the startup optimization hints were gated behind a `platform === "linux"` check, which meant:
- macOS users running on ARM64 (M1/M2/M3) did not receive startup optimization guidance
- macOS users with low memory (<8GB) also did not receive guidance
- Memory information was available (via `os.totalmem()`) but never used for macOS

## Solution

Modified `noteStartupOptimizationHints` to:
1. Support both Linux and Darwin platforms
2. Renamed `isLowMemoryLinux` to `isLowMemory` (platform-agnostic)
3. Updated the `isStartupTuneTarget` check to include Darwin
4. Added comprehensive test coverage for macOS scenarios (ARM64, low-memory, high-memory)

## Changes

- `src/commands/doctor-platform-notes.ts`: Extended platform check and improved variable naming
- `src/commands/doctor-platform-notes.startup-optimization.test.ts`: Added 3 new test cases for Darwin platform

## Testing

Added tests for:
- Darwin with ARM64 (Apple Silicon)
- Darwin with low memory (<8GB)
- Darwin with high memory and non-ARM arch (should skip)

Fixes #47273

target.title: fix: respect totalTokensFresh flag to avoid showing stale token counts
target.body:
## Problem
When a session's `totalTokensFresh` flag is `false`, the `totalTokens` value may be stale/historical (e.g., accumulated from previous compaction) and should not be displayed.

This caused incorrect displays like "100% context used 2.6M / 200k" in both TUI and Web UI when the token count was stale.

## Root Cause
The Gateway already correctly filters stale token counts via `resolveFreshSessionTotalTokens`, but:
1. **TUI**: Type definition was missing `totalTokensFresh` field, so TUI couldn't check it
2. **UI**: `formatSessionTokens` function didn't check the freshness flag

## Solution

### TUI (commit 368ca03f1)
- Add `totalTokensFresh` to `GatewaySessionList` type in `gateway-chat.ts`
- Add `totalTokensFresh` to `SessionInfoEntry` type in `tui-session-actions.ts`
- Check `totalTokensFresh !== false` before updating `sessionInfo.totalTokens`

### UI (commit 446fdd13a)
- Add `totalTokensFresh` to `GatewaySessionRow` type in `types.ts`
- Check `totalTokensFresh === false` in `formatSessionTokens` function, return "n/a" when stale

## Testing
- TypeScript compilation passes for both TUI and UI
- The fix ensures stale token counts are not displayed in sessions list

## Files Changed
- `src/tui/gateway-chat.ts` - Add `totalTokensFresh` to type definition
- `src/tui/tui-session-actions.ts` - Add type and check before using `totalTokens`
- `ui/src/ui/types.ts` - Add `totalTokensFresh` to type definition
- `ui/src/ui/presenter.ts` - Check freshness before displaying tokens

target.title: feat(feishu): enable real-time character-by-character streaming for subagent runs
target.body:
## AI Disclosure
Built with Antigravity (powered by Gemini) and refined with Claude.  
Ran AI review passes as a sanity check before submitting.  

Fully tested — verified real-time streaming for both main agent and subagent lanes.

---

## Summary

### Problem
Previously, character-by-character streaming was only optimized for the main agent run. When a subagent was spawned, its output would often arrive as a single bulk message or be delayed, leading to a “frozen” user experience during complex background tasks.

### Why it matters
Subagents are core to complex background task execution. A lack of real-time feedback makes it hard for users to track progress, causing UX frustration. Good observability is essential for trust in agentic workflows.

### What changed (Core Feat: Subagent Streaming Bridge)

- **Architectural Hook**  
  Introduced `createOutboundDispatcher` to the `ChannelPlugin` interface.  
  This allows subagent runs to tap into the same real-time UI streaming infrastructure as the main agent.

- **Feishu Implementation**  
  Bridged subagent output directly to `FeishuStreamingSession`.  
  Subagents now stream text character-by-character with parity to the main agent.

- **Advanced UI Polish**
  - **Tool Animations**: Dynamic status lines showing which tool is active (e.g., `💻 exec`, `🤖 sessions_spawn`, `🌍 web_search`)
  - **Reasoning Process**: Real-time display of the "Thinking" block within the Feishu card
  - **UX Enhancements**: Integrated rich iconography (emojis) for all standard tool mappings

---

## Technical Details

- **Sync with Upstream**  
  Fully rebased onto the latest `upstream/main`, adapting to the new asynchronous `markDispatchIdle` and `replaceContent` interfaces.

- **Atomic Rendering**  
  Switched to `replaceContent` in Feishu to ensure clean transitions between tool status, reasoning blocks, and final answers (preventing UI ghosting).

- **Clean History**  
  Consolidated into a single, clean feature commit to maintain repository health.

---

## Verification Plan

### Automated Tests
Verified TypeScript build/lint success:

```bash
npm run lint
npm run type-check
```

### Manual Verification

- **Subagent Streaming**  
  Spawned subagents in a Feishu group chat and verified that the output streams character-by-character.

- **Tool UI**  
  Confirmed that the "Thinking" and tool-specific emojis display correctly during execution.

- **Concurrency**  
  Verified that multiple streaming cards (main + subagent) can be managed simultaneously without state collision.

target.title: feat(cli): add trust windows for time-bounded exec approval
target.body:
## AI Disclosure

Built with Claude and GPT-5.3 Codex. Ran AI review passes as a sanity check before submitting. Fully tested — 64 new test cases covering the main flows and auth edge cases.

---

## Summary

- **Problem:** Right now it's all-or-nothing for exec approvals: either approve every command individually, or turn off approvals entirely. When you're doing a long build or batch refactor you already trust, the per-command prompts get old fast.
- **Why it matters:** There's no safe middle ground. People who get tired of prompts just disable approvals.  Good security is usable security.  Using a time-bound system is a pattern common to many other types of escalation systems (sudo, access tokens, etc.)
- **What changed:** Three new CLI commands (`trust`, `untrust`, `trust-status`) that let an operator open a time-limited window where commands run without prompts. In-memory trust state, one enforcement point in `resolveExecApprovalsFromFile`, JSONL audit trail of commands that ran during the window, caller auth checks.
- **What did NOT change:** The existing exec approval pipeline is untouched — the trust override is additive, bolted on at the end of `resolveExecApprovalsFromFile`. No changes to auth, tokens, network calls, or wire protocol. Agents can't initiate trust windows; only interactive CLI sessions can.  This PR doesn't expose this feature through common usage points like TUI, Discord, Whatsapp, etc.  It does lay a foundation for those features to be added next.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #46657 (earlier iteration with a messier commit history — will close when this merges)

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- **New commands:**
  - `openclaw approvals trust --minutes N` — opens a trust window (default cap: 60m, up to 480m with `--force`)
  - `openclaw approvals untrust` — revokes the window and prints what commands ran
  - `openclaw approvals trust-status` — shows whether a window is active and how much time is left
- While a trust window is active, commands for that agent run without approval prompts (`security=full`, `ask=off`).
- Trust state is in-memory only. Restarting the gateway clears everything.
- A JSONL audit log is written to `~/.openclaw/trust-audit-<agentId>.jsonl` during the window (mode 0o600). `untrust` deletes it by default; `--keep-audit` preserves it.
- All three commands require an interactive CLI terminal. Agent sessions and non-CLI clients are rejected.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? **Yes**
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? **No**
- New/changed network calls? **No** — new RPC methods go over the existing WebSocket connection
- Command/tool execution surface changed? **Yes** — during a trust window, commands skip the approval prompt
- Data access scope changed? **No**

**Risk + mitigation:**

This is a privilege escalation feature, so it needs to be locked down:

- **Who can grant:** Only interactive CLI sessions with device identity (or loopback + token/password auth). Agents can't grant trust to themselves — `requireNonAgentSession` checks both `OPENCLAW_SESSION_KEY` and `OPENCLAW_AGENT_ID`.
- **How long:** 60m default cap. You need `--force` to go higher, and 480m is a hard ceiling.
- **How many:** You can't stack windows. One per agent, period. Revoke first if you want a new one. Importantly, this prevents re-entry or extension by an agent.
- **What's recorded:** JSONL audit of every command that ran, plus gateway structured logs on grant/revoke. The audit is best-effort (won't fail exec on I/O errors), but the gateway logs are always there.
- **What persists:** Nothing. In-memory only. Process restart = clean slate.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS / Linux / Windows
- Runtime/container: Node.js 22+
- Model/provider: N/A (infrastructure change)
- Integration/channel: CLI → Gateway WebSocket
- Relevant config: `~/.openclaw/exec-approvals.json` (unchanged format)

### Steps

1. Start the gateway: `openclaw gateway start`
2. Grant a trust window: `openclaw approvals trust --minutes 10 --yes`
3. Run commands — they execute without approval prompts
4. Check status: `openclaw approvals trust-status`
5. Revoke: `openclaw approvals untrust --yes`

### Expected

- Step 2: Prints trust window active with duration and expiry time
- Step 3: Commands run without prompts
- Step 4: Shows `active` with remaining minutes
- Step 5: Prints revocation confirmation with audit summary

### Actual

- Matches expected.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

64 tests across 8 files (1,100+ lines of test code). Coverage includes:
- Grant/revoke lifecycle, duration caps, anti-stacking, expired windows
- Caller auth: device identity, loopback auth, non-CLI rejection, agent-session blocking (both env vars)
- Audit: append, load, summarize, cleanup, malformed JSONL resilience, empty commands, boundary formatting
- CLI: `--force`, `--keep-audit`, `--minutes` flag propagation, TTY checks
- Exec integration: audit entries on gateway/node success and error paths

Happy-path coverage is solid. Edge cases around real device-identity auth are tested via mocked `GatewayClient` objects, not live auth flows.

## Human Verification (required)

- **Verified scenarios:** Trust grant → command execution without prompts → status check → revoke with audit summary. Rejection from non-TTY, agent sessions, and non-CLI callers. Anti-stacking. Duration cap enforcement.
- **Edge cases checked:** Malformed JSONL lines (skipped), audit write failure (logged, doesn't break exec), `--force` with durations above 60m, idempotent untrust when no window exists, revoke after natural expiry.
- **What I did not verify:** Load testing with many concurrent trust windows. Real device-identity auth (mocked in tests). Swift protocol model compilation on macOS.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? **Yes** — all new code. Older clients work identically.
- Config/env changes? **No**
- Migration needed? **No**

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- **How to disable/revert:** Revert this PR. Trust state is in-memory — restarting the gateway clears everything. No persisted state to clean up.
- **Files/config to restore:** None. Audit files in `~/.openclaw/` can be safely deleted.
- **Known bad symptoms:** If the trust override in `resolveExecApprovalsFromFile` misbehaves, commands could bypass approval prompts when they shouldn't. Watch for `trustWindowActive: true` in resolved approvals when no window was granted.

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** User grants a long trust window and walks away — agent has elevated permissions for up to 8 hours.
  - **Mitigation:** Default cap is 60 minutes. `--force` required for longer. 480m hard max. Audit trail + gateway logs record everything.

- **Risk:** Audit trail is best-effort — I/O errors don't fail exec, so there could be gaps.
  - **Mitigation:** Gateway structured logs record grant/revoke events regardless. The audit file is a nice-to-have; the gateway logs are the real safety net.
